title: Solving the diamond with virtual inheritance
prompt_md:
## Goal

The diamond problem: `B` and `C` both inherit from `A`, and `D` inherits from both. With normal inheritance `D` would contain two separate `A` subobjects, so state stored in `A` would be duplicated. Use **virtual inheritance** so that `D` holds exactly one shared `A`, then prove the sharing: a change made through the `B` path is visible through the `C` path because they touch the same counter.

Build classes `A`, `B`, `C`, `D`. `A` holds an `int` counter initialized by the most-derived class. `B` provides `addViaB(int)` and `C` provides `addViaC(int)`, each adding its argument to the shared counter. `D` is the most-derived class and must initialize the single shared `A` directly. Expose the current counter via a `getCount()` accessor on `D`.

## Input

- Line 1: three integers `start b c`.

## Output

Starting the counter at `start`, apply `addViaB(b)`, then `addViaC(c)`, on one `D` object, printing the counter after each step:
```
via B: <counter after addViaB>
via C: <counter after addViaC>
shared: <final counter>
```
If `A` were duplicated, the `B` and `C` additions would land on different counters; with a virtual base they accumulate on one.

Example input `10 5 7`:
```
via B: 15
via C: 22
shared: 22
```

title: Constructor responsibility in virtual inheritance
prompt_md:
## Goal

When a class has a virtual base, only the **most-derived class's** initialization of that base takes effect -- the values that intermediate classes try to pass to the virtual base are *ignored*. You will make this observable: each intermediate class records what it actually *sees* in the shared base at the moment it is constructed.

Build a virtual-inheritance diamond: `B` and `C` each virtually inherit from `A`, and `D` inherits from both. `A` stores an `int id`. `B`'s constructor lists `A{bId}` in its initializer list (its *attempt* to set the base) and also takes a `tag`; in that same initializer list it computes `observed = id + tag`, capturing the base id it can see. `C` does the same with `cId` and its own tag. `D`, the most-derived class, initializes `A` directly with `dId`.

Because the single virtual base `A` is constructed **once, by `D`, before `B` and `C` run**, both `B` and `C` see `id == dId` -- *not* the `bId`/`cId` they passed. So each `observed` is `dId + tag`, proving the intermediate base-initializations were ignored. (A naive non-virtual diamond would give two separate `A`s, and `B`/`C` would instead see `bId`/`cId`, producing different numbers.)

## Input

- Line 1: five integers `bId cId dId bTag cTag`.

## Output

```
A id: <dId>
B observed: <dId + bTag>
C observed: <dId + cTag>
```

Example input `1 2 99 10 20`:
```
A id: 99
B observed: 109
C observed: 119
```
Note `B observed` is `99 + 10`, using `dId` (99), **not** `bId` (1) -- B's own `A{1}` was ignored.

test_cases.label: D wins; B and C observe dId not bId/cId
test_cases.expected_stdout:
A id: 99
B observed: 109
C observed: 119

test_cases.label: Distinct values
test_cases.expected_stdout:
A id: 7
B observed: 15
C observed: 16

test_cases.label: All zero
test_cases.expected_stdout:
A id: 0
B observed: 0
C observed: 0

test_cases.label: Negatives
test_cases.expected_stdout:
A id: -3
B observed: -7
C observed: -8
